original_hypothesis.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Has Zero Fee Enabling Free Capital for Atomic Exploits
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: executeFlashLoanERC20 transfers tokens out and requires the same amount returned, but charges zero fee. The flash loan validation (validateFlashLoanERC20Basic) checks only basic params and asset type. Combined with the reentrancy in flashLoanERC20, an attacker gets free capital for any atomic strategy: oracle manipulation, liquidation arbitrage, or cross-protocol exploitation. The absence of fees means there is no economic friction deterring flash loan abuse.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Most lending protocols charge flash loan fees (e.g., Aave charges 0.09%). Zero-fee flash loans reduce the cost of every atomic exploit to only gas costs, making borderline-profitable attacks viable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan sends exact amounts; erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan pulls back exact same amounts; no premium/fee calculation or enforcement

preconditions: Flash loans enabled for target asset (assetData.isFlashLoanEnabled)
Sufficient available liquidity in the pool
Attacker has a profitable atomic strategy requiring capital

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan(receiverAddress, assets, amounts) - same amounts as transfer out, zero premium
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Free flash loans enable zero-cost capital for any atomic exploit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All flash-loan-dependent attacks become viable at zero marginal cost

economic_questions: Was the zero fee intentional design or oversight?
What is the maximum flash loanable amount per asset?
Combined with reentrancy, what specific exploits become profitable at zero fee?
validation_requirements: Verify no fee is charged by executing a flash loan and checking balances
Calculate profit thresholds for various exploit strategies with zero vs typical fees
Check if fee-on-transfer tokens could result in loss to pool on return

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Variable; any atomic strategy profiting more than gas costs is viable
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on combined strategy; zero fee removes capital cost barrier

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Pool's available liquidity determines maximum flash loan size
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Combined with reentrancy window for maximum impact
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Enables any flash-loan-dependent attack at minimal cost

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Suppliers (no fee income from flash loans); protocol (no revenue)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on downstream exploits enabled by free capital

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Per-asset available liquidity for max flash loan sizing
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Execute flash loan, verify zero premium collected

validation_result.impact_summary: The zero-fee flash loan is a design choice, not a code defect. The code correctly transfers out and transfers back the exact same amounts with no premium logic present, missing, or broken.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis claims that the absence of a flash loan fee in executeFlashLoanERC20 constitutes a code defect. After tracing the complete code path, the flash loan implementation correctly: (1) validates parameters via validateFlashLoanERC20Basic, (2) transfers exact amounts out via erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan, (3) executes the receiver callback, and (4) pulls back the exact same amounts via erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan. There is no fee variable, fee parameter, fee calculation, or commented-out fee code anywhere in the flash loan path. The IFlashLoanReceiver interface does not include a premiums parameter (unlike Aave's equivalent). The isFlashLoanEnabled per-asset flag shows this is a deliberately configurable feature. Zero-fee flash loans are a legitimate design pattern used by Uniswap V3, dYdX, and others. The code is not wrong - it correctly implements the intended behavior. The cited invariant INV-ECON-006 states 'The pool's available liquidity must not decrease after a flash loan execution' which is SATISFIED by the zero-fee implementation since the same amounts are returned.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: FlashLoanLogic.sol lines 18-50 show the complete flash loan flow. The same inputParams.amounts array is used for both erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan (line 31) and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan (line 43). VaultLogic.sol erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan uses safeTransfer(to, amounts[i]) and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan uses safeTransferFrom(from, address(this), amounts[i]) with identical amounts. No fee computation exists anywhere in the codebase for flash loans. This is consistent across all analyzed contracts.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: NOT a vulnerability. The code correctly implements zero-fee flash loans. The same amounts array is used for both transfer out and transfer in, which is the intended behavior. No fee logic is present, missing, or broken.

calling_sequence: ValidateLogic.validateFlashLoanERC20Basic (validates pool, array lengths, receiver address)
IFlashLoanReceiver.executeOperationERC20(assets, amounts, msgSender, pool, params) - callback with no premiums parameter
VaultLogic.erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan(receiverAddress, assets, amounts) - pulls back exact same amounts
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entire flash loan path is consistent with a zero-fee design. erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan (VaultLogic.sol) calls safeTransfer(to, amounts[i]) for each asset. erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan calls safeTransferFrom(from, address(this), amounts[i]) with the same amounts. No availableLiquidity is modified during the flash loan. The IFlashLoanReceiver callback interface doesn't include a premiums array, confirming zero-fee was the design intent. This is not a defective code path.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No flash loan fee configuration exists in deployment scripts. The InitConfigPool.s.sol script configures asset parameters but no flash loan fee parameter is set, consistent with the design having no fee mechanism at all.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The hypothesis conflates a design choice with a code defect. Zero-fee flash loans are used by multiple major protocols (Uniswap V3, dYdX). While the absence of fees reduces the economic cost of flash-loan-dependent attacks, this is an economic design trade-off, not a code bug. The per-asset isFlashLoanEnabled flag (checked at FlashLoanLogic.sol line 27-28) shows the feature is deliberately configurable. The actual security concern with flash loans in this protocol is the missing nonReentrant modifier (a separate, already-identified issue INV-STATE-001), not the absence of fees.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No code defect exists. The zero-fee design may result in lost fee revenue for suppliers and lower economic barriers for flash-loan-dependent strategies, but these are design trade-offs rather than bugs. The pool balance is correctly conserved (same amount out and in). The invariant INV-ECON-006 ('pool's available liquidity must not decrease after flash loan') is satisfied.
recommended_mitigations: Consider adding a configurable flash loan premium (e.g., 0.05-0.09%) as a revenue source for liquidity providers
Add pre/post balance checks in erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan to protect against fee-on-transfer tokens (this IS a separate real issue)
Document the zero-fee design choice explicitly to distinguish it from an oversight
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy protocol with a test ERC20 asset with flash loans enabled. 2. Execute a flash loan for X tokens. 3. In the callback, verify X tokens received. 4. Return exactly X tokens. 5. Verify transaction succeeds and pool balance is unchanged. 6. This confirms zero-fee is the intended behavior.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol team intended to add fees later (no code comments or TODO markers found)
Whether a separate fee mechanism exists in an unseen contract (e.g., Configurator could set flash loan fees not visible in provided code)
additional_testing_needed: Verify no flash loan fee parameter exists in the Configurator module (not provided in scope)
Test fee-on-transfer token behavior with flash loans (separate potential issue: erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan lacks balance delta check unlike erc20TransferInLiquidity)
